chapters.description: A long silent stretch deepens the practice, including the reminder that nothing needs to be acquired, proved, or become anyone.

chapters.title: Give up ownership
chapters.description: The Buddha reference to giving up what is not yours opens into a repeated inquiry about ownership, answered with 'everything.'

chapters.title: Stillness as object
chapters.description: The practice turns to stillness directly, with repeated instructions to take stillness as the object and not go anywhere.

chapters.title: Closing and transition
chapters.description: The meditation ends by easing out of formal practice while staying with the body, the visual field, and a non-grasping attitude.

title: Talk: Whys and Hows of Effort - Energy & Bhava Tanha
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What kind of effort is helpful in practice, and when does striving quietly become another form of craving? The talk moves from trust and patience, through motivation shaped by benefit to others, into the subtle ways progress-seeking can tighten the mind around becoming and achievement. Along the way, it draws on Sharon Salzberg, Nietzsche, Suzuki Roshi, Ajahn Sumedho, and the Tibetan reflections on precious human life to show how energy can come from receptivity rather than force.
short_summary: A close look at effort in practice, tracing the shift from striving and progress to trust, receptivity, and the subtler cravings hidden inside spiritual ambition.

chapters.title: Listening and trust
chapters.description: He opens by reflecting on how to listen to Dharma talks selectively, then turns to effort, patience, and trust as the mood that lets practice take root.

chapters.title: Practice that simply continues
chapters.description: Using Sharon Salzberg and Nietzsche, he describes practice as something we keep doing from faith in the path, even when results are not obvious.

chapters.title: Why make the effort
chapters.description: He asks what actually sustains effort, shifts from self-centered motivation to concern for others, and points to the way healing and love spill beyond the self.

chapters.title: Precious human life
chapters.description: He introduces the Tibetan contemplations, beginning with the preciousness and strangeness of being born at all as a source of reverence and motivation.

chapters.title: Against the stream
chapters.description: A long aside on cosmology gives way to the Buddhist point that conditioning pushes us toward habit, while practice asks for a critical stance and real energy to let go.

chapters.title: Open palm of release
chapters.description: He explores how effort becomes the energy to listen to our own wisdom, then turns toward receptivity, surrender, and willingness rather than willfulness.

chapters.title: Non-gaining practice
chapters.description: Suzuki Roshi's teaching on non-gaining practice frames the danger of spiritual ambition and the subtle forms of craving that hide inside progress.

chapters.title: Your own path
chapters.description: He ends with a brief silence and a closing reminder to take what is useful, leave the rest, and let the path feel like one's own even within the Buddhist tradition.

title: Guided Meditation: Being Held, Settling In
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What if the first task is simply to let yourself be held—by rain, by the earth, by the fact of being here? The talk begins with that sense of support and then moves into a guided settling with the breath, noticing the cycle of in-breath and out-breath, false alarms of urgency, and the ways suffering can be met without obeying it. Later it turns toward walking practice, boredom, and the quiet possibility that goodness is building even when little seems to be happening.
short_summary: A guided meditation on resting in being held, working with breath, urgency, and walking practice as ways of settling into presence.

chapters.title: Feeling Held
chapters.description: He begins with rain and the sense of being held by the world, inviting a bodily experience of support.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: The instruction turns toward making peace with imperfection and resting in what is subtly pleasant.

chapters.title: Letting Go Of Time
chapters.description: He asks listeners to release orientation to retreat days and fall more fully into the present.

chapters.title: Breath Cycle
chapters.description: Attention is gathered on the breath, including the beginning and end of inhalation and exhalation and the brief release at the bottom.

chapters.title: False Alarms
chapters.description: He names the flood of signals in body and mind and suggests treating phenomena as false alarms rather than commands.

chapters.title: Working With Urgency
chapters.description: A whirlpool of urgency is met with recognition, blessing, and breathing until the pull loosens and breathing is available again.

chapters.title: Suffering As Dukkha
chapters.description: If suffering arises, he reframes the moment as an occasion to realize dukkha more clearly.

chapters.title: Walking Meditation
chapters.description: The talk shifts to walking practice, explaining posture, open eyes, visual stimulation, boredom, and the quiet accrual of goodness.

chapters.title: Fresh Anchors
chapters.description: He closes by encouraging variation in the walking anchor, creative freshness, and a final trust in the logic of Dharma.

title: Guided Meditation: Peace is Close
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: A quiet guided practice begins with a few deeper breaths, then asks us to make room for worries without getting pulled into them. The talk repeatedly returns to the gesture of offering the heart to this moment, laying down the project of life, and breathing without elaboration. As the practice settles, it turns toward subtle shifts in attention, the softening of self-construction, and a question of what remains when we stop taking ourselves as an object.
short_summary: A guided meditation that moves from settling the breath to laying down defenses, softening self-construction, and resting in simple presence.

chapters.title: Arriving Together
chapters.description: He welcomes the group and invites them to practice without needing to explain much.

chapters.title: Landing In Breath
chapters.description: The meditation begins with deeper breaths and a simple settling into the body.

chapters.title: Putting Worries Down
chapters.description: He asks listeners to make room for worries without abandoning them, and to stand defenseless before the moment.

chapters.title: Offering The Heart
chapters.description: The instruction becomes repeated and plain: lay down life-projects and offer the heart to what is here.

chapters.title: Breathing Without Elaboration
chapters.description: He explores how experience gets elaborated and points back to the simplicity of just being a body breathing.

chapters.title: Small Shifts Matter
chapters.description: He acknowledges how hard practice can feel, then points to subtle, nearby shifts that can be noticed and appreciated.

chapters.title: Constructing Self
chapters.description: The talk turns to how selfing is built from quiet assumptions and unnoticed sense data.

chapters.title: Unseen And Empty
chapters.description: He invites a contemplation of never being seen as an object and closes with the image of empty phenomena rolling on.

title: Dharmette: Fear at the Gateway of Stillness
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Fear is traced to the edge of stillness: just as the mind begins to settle, a habit of vigilance rushes in and insists that surrender is unsafe. The talk follows that tension from body-level fear to the deeper unease around uncertainty, not-self, and the thinning of the usual sense of control. Rather than pushing through by force, it points toward making fear an object of practice, habituating to the edge, and letting faith and trust carry the moment when silence feels risky. Along the way, the speaker grounds the discussion in ordinary life pressures, then widens it to mortality, grief, and the possibility that nothing hidden is waiting to ambush us.
short_summary: A reflection on how fear arises at the gateway of stillness and how practice can meet it with awareness, patience, and trust.

chapters.title: The question of fear
chapters.description: Matthew introduces a listener's question about fear arising in meditation and names the basic problem.

chapters.title: Safety and vigilance
chapters.description: He explores how survival conditioning, caretaking pressure, and cultural expectations can make vigilance feel necessary.

chapters.title: Why letting go feels unsafe
chapters.description: The talk turns to the way clinging, thinking, and worrying can feel like safety when uncertainty is present.

chapters.title: At the gateway
chapters.description: Matthew describes how deeper stillness can trigger the sense that one must do something, even though willfulness only adds contraction.

chapters.title: Spiritual vertigo
chapters.description: He names the jarring quality of attenuated self-states and the feeling of standing near an abyss as the ordinary self thins out.

chapters.title: Working with fear
chapters.description: Using the image of being 'on the cusp,' he suggests releasing the need for something to happen and making fear an object of clear awareness.

chapters.title: Training the nervous system
chapters.description: He notes that practice is often gradual, aimed at de-electrifying the past so old pain no longer ambushes us.

chapters.title: Mortality and faith
chapters.description: With references to Ajahn Sucitto and Suzuki Roshi, he shifts from hidden monsters to mortality, change, and the possibility of faith.

chapters.title: Surrender to silence
chapters.description: The talk ends by inviting trust in Dharma and lineage, and by framing silence as something that beckons rather than threatens.

title: Guided Meditation: Sangha & Self
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What if the thing that blocks waking up is the feeling that we should be able to do it alone? This guided meditation begins with a quiet reflection on Sangha as something essential rather than secondary, using Socratic dialogue and the “jewel of Sangha” to point toward mutual dependence, shared inquiry, and the relief of not having to hold everything by oneself. The talk then settles into practice, softening into breath and collective goodwill while noticing how self-view shapes what seems solid, until even the sense of “I am” is treated as something that can be seen, loosened, and made empty.
short_summary: A guided meditation on Sangha, mutual dependence, and the way shared practice can soften self-view and the sense of being alone.

chapters.title: Opening and context
chapters.description: Matthew greets the group, responds briefly to a question about substitute teachers, and explains why the evening will focus on community and practice together.

chapters.title: Open Socrates
chapters.description: He introduces Agnes Callard’s Open Socrates and uses its argument about needing others to think well as a bridge into Dharma practice.

chapters.title: The jewel of Sangha
chapters.description: He names Sangha as a true jewel, equal to Buddha and Dharma, and invites the group to feel the goodness of collective practice before settling in.

chapters.title: Mutual dependence
chapters.description: The meditation begins with breath and goodwill, emphasizing that we cannot wake up alone and that we stand on one another’s shoulders.

chapters.title: Seeing self-view
chapters.description: The teaching turns toward self-models, self-view, and the way another person can help reveal assumptions that are usually hidden.

chapters.title: Noticing the vantage point
chapters.description: Matthew points to the ways experience is organized around a presumed vantage point and encourages dis-identifying from it through careful noticing.

chapters.title: Subtle images and words
chapters.description: The close narrows in on the subtle feeling, words, and images that become the center of gravity of 'I am-ness' when unnoticed, and empty when seen.

title: Dharmette: Moral outrage and love
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: How do love and moral outrage get tangled, and can anger ever be useful without hardening into hate? The talk begins with ordinary care and widens into a question about how our concern for suffering can become fused with disgust, punishment, fear, and self-view. Drawing on Ruth King and Hannah Chapman, it follows that tension toward a quieter claim: outrage may start the movement, but love has to have the last word, even if that means carrying more grief and less certainty.
short_summary: A meditation on how moral outrage, disgust, and fear can entangle with care, and how practice tries to untwist them back toward love.

chapters.title: Love before practice
chapters.description: He begins with the depth of care already present in ordinary life and suggests that practice can widen and free that love.

chapters.title: Outrage and entanglement
chapters.description: The talk turns to moral outrage, the question of whether it can be trusted, and Ruth King’s reminder that anger can begin action but not complete it.

chapters.title: When care turns fierce
chapters.description: He explores how love can become yoked with rage, punishment, and revulsion, and asks how to use moral intensity without corroding the heart.

chapters.title: Purifying moral emotion
chapters.description: Drawing on Ruth King and his own uncertainty, he distinguishes useful engagement from the distortions of clinging, hatred, and the wish to force change.

chapters.title: Disgust and hate
chapters.description: A cited passage from Hannah Chapman helps frame moral disgust as a borrowed, ancient withdrawal response that can blur into hatred.

chapters.title: Fear and self-view
chapters.description: He emphasizes learning to surf strong moral emotions, take care of fear, and notice when moral commitment becomes egoic tribalism.

chapters.title: Love without a self
chapters.description: The talk deepens into self-view, empty processes, and the tendency to attach hatred to identity markers rather than seeing clearly.

chapters.title: Grief as the burden
chapters.description: He closes by saying moral sanity is kept through love, even if that means more grief, and that tears can become a source of motivation.

title: Guided Meditation: Abiding in Goodness
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What happens when kindness and compassion are treated not as extras, but as necessities? This guided meditation keeps returning to that question by inviting a direct reconnection with love, especially when the heart feels dry, brittle, or tired by hatred and misattunement. The practice moves from resting in an image, word, or felt sense of love, to recognizing the shared longing of all beings to be well, and finally to a body-based balance of tenderness and courage. Along the way, the talk leans on the phrases "strong back, soft front" and the idea that love sometimes feels warm and bright, and sometimes simply makes space for whatever needs to be felt.
short_summary: A guided meditation on abiding in love as an essential nourishment, balancing softness and courage, and meeting whatever arises with care.

chapters.title: Love as Necessity
chapters.description: The talk opens by asking what it means to treat kindness and compassion as essential nourishment rather than optional comfort.

chapters.title: Abiding in Love
chapters.description: Matthew turns toward sangha and invites listeners to settle into an image, phrase, or felt sense of love and remain there.

chapters.title: Self and Others
chapters.description: The practice widens into the recognition that every movement of mind and body is a search for happiness, leading into compassion for oneself and others.

chapters.title: Grief and Courage
chapters.description: He notes that contact with goodness can feel like grief or ecstasy, then introduces Joan Halifax’s "strong back, soft front" as an embodied balance.

chapters.title: Deep Faith in Love
chapters.description: The meditation settles into the sense that love may feel tender or may simply allow whatever needs to be felt, both expressing confidence in love.

chapters.title: Closing Refrain
chapters.description: He repeats the body-based image of softness and strength, ending with the reminder that love may feel like love or may simply make room for experience.

title: Dharmette: On not understanding dharma talks
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: When a Dharma talk feels out of reach, is that a sign you missed something—or part of the practice itself? The talk begins with an anonymous question about moments of recognition and moments of confusion, then moves into a gentle defense of not understanding, arguing that wisdom includes noticing the limits of one’s own view. Along the way, it draws on the Buddha’s teaching style, Agnes Callard on Socratic inquiry, and Socrates as someone who cared more about learning than being right. The close is plainspoken: there are no makeup classes, only patience, ripening, and the willingness to let understanding come in its own time.
short_summary: A quiet reflection on why not understanding a Dharma talk can be part of learning, with patience, humility, and Socratic inquiry as guides.

chapters.title: The listener's question
chapters.description: Matthew reads the anonymous question about talks that feel clear versus talks that seem out of reach.

chapters.title: Teaching is imperfect
chapters.description: He reflects on the difficulty of teaching Dharma at all and the many editorial choices teachers make.

chapters.title: Recognition and intimacy
chapters.description: He turns to sincerity, the feeling of remembering, and the intimacy that can arise when a teacher speaks plainly to one's mind.

chapters.title: Wisdom in not knowing
chapters.description: Matthew says that recognizing what one does not understand is itself wise, and uses Agnes Callard to frame the challenge of questioning our own assumptions.

chapters.title: Learning to be wrong
chapters.description: He develops the theme of doubt, overconfidence, and the willingness to be surprised, drawing on Socrates as an example.

chapters.title: Practice matures
chapters.description: Matthew looks back on years of practice, describing a shift from dramatic discovery to a steadier but still vulnerable relation to Dharma.

chapters.title: Patience and belief
chapters.description: He ends by encouraging patience, saying there are no makeup classes for wisdom and that understanding often comes later than trust.

title: Equanimity 6
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What changes when the heart stops wrestling with pleasant and unpleasant, and letting go is no longer something to do? The talk moves from the hard work of cultivating equanimity, through the stage of “equanimity with formations,” into a deeper sense of clarity where nothing sticks and experience feels less dualistic. Along the way, the speaker weighs the wholesomeness of this freedom against the risks of dissociation, nihilism, and mistaking states for liberation, then returns to the steadier question of what actually supports a Dharma life.
short_summary: A quiet exploration of equanimity that moves from effortful practice to a deeper, less grasping freedom, while asking what keeps that freedom grounded and humane.

chapters.title: Cultivating Equanimity
chapters.description: He begins by distinguishing the gradual training of equanimity from a more ripened expression of it, emphasizing the work and energy the practice can require.

chapters.title: Beyond Forcing Letting Go
chapters.description: Matthew describes how practice can move from wrestling with pleasant and unpleasant toward a quieter state where letting go is no longer forced.

chapters.title: Mahasi Equanimity
chapters.description: He explains the Mahasi tradition’s account of equanimity with formations, where awareness seems to observe itself and the mind becomes extremely clear.

chapters.title: The Three Characteristics
chapters.description: The talk turns to how dukkha, impermanence, and not-self appear vividly in this phase, with experience feeling more neutral, coreless, and less personal.

chapters.title: Safety In Groundlessness
chapters.description: Matthew reflects on the relief and security found when nothing sticks to the mind, while also noting how this can resemble dissociation if it goes in the wrong direction.

chapters.title: Rumi And Letting Go
chapters.description: He uses Rumi’s language of living in the nowhere you come from to point toward equanimity, then cites the Buddha on safety being found in relinquishment.

chapters.title: Compassion And Nihilism
chapters.description: He considers how this kind of release can deepen compassion rather than nihilism, and how it changes one’s relation to the drama of suffering.

chapters.title: Not Chasing States
chapters.description: Drawing on Shinzen Young, Matthew warns against making spiritual practice into a quest for special experiences and says mature practice becomes less bound up with states.

chapters.title: A Dharma Life
chapters.description: He closes by admitting it is hard to trace exactly what causes freedom, then lands on the sense that practice becomes an ongoing Dharma life.

title: Equanimity 5
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What does equanimity look like when life and practice refuse to fit a clean story? The talk begins by questioning neat spiritual autobiographies, then moves into a practical, almost clinical look at how equanimity is noticed, lost, and slowly restored in the midst of thought, clinging, and emotional overload. It lingers on the limits of what can be done in the moment, the way self-stories drain their charge, and how pain tolerance, care for others, and remorse can support a steadier heart.
short_summary: A grounded talk that questions neat spiritual narratives and turns to the practical work of recognizing when the mind is caught, respecting limits, and building equanimity through pain, care, and less charged self-story.

chapters.title: Unreliable spiritual stories
chapters.description: Brensilver questions the neat stories people tell about their own practice and why they cannot fully explain a life of Dharma practice.

chapters.title: Sincerity over certainty
chapters.description: He briefly invokes Suzuki Roshi to suggest that sincerity matters more than a clean yes-or-no idea of enlightenment.

chapters.title: Noticing lack of equanimity
chapters.description: He shifts to equanimity as something you can notice when you are caught, much as you notice a thought or trance.

chapters.title: Equanimity and not-self
chapters.description: The talk develops the mutual relationship between equanimity and not-self, especially through loosening the emotional charge around self-stories.

chapters.title: Draining self-charges
chapters.description: He gives everyday examples of letting identity stories rattle until their affect drains out, making the heart less brittle and reactive.

chapters.title: Respecting limits
chapters.description: Brensilver emphasizes practical limits: when the mind is gripped or depleted, the skillful move may simply be to stop, rest, or say no.

chapters.title: Expanding pain tolerance
chapters.description: He describes practice as gradually increasing tolerance for pain and reducing its unpleasantness, rather than eliminating sensation itself.

chapters.title: Holding pain for others
chapters.description: The talk ends by linking equanimity to compassion, solidarity, and the wish to be safe for others when our own regulation falters.

title: Equanimity 4
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What happens when compassion reaches the end of its power? The talk follows a practice of alternating loving-kindness and equanimity, especially when caring becomes heavy, clingy, or ineffective, and when the heart needs to rest rather than strain to fix what it cannot control. A friend’s month-long heart practice, reflections on global suffering, and a brief reference to Carl Rogers all help shape the teaching into a practical rhythm: take in suffering, respond with love, then return to the recognition of limits and release.
short_summary: A talk on balancing compassion and equanimity when care meets helplessness, with a simple back-and-forth practice of opening, holding, and letting go.

chapters.title: Opening practice frame
chapters.description: He introduces walking practice and an alternating heart practice that moves between metta and equanimity.

chapters.title: Love at the limit
chapters.description: The talk turns to what love feels like when our wishes do not govern outcomes and control falls away.

chapters.title: Holding suffering lightly
chapters.description: He describes feeling another's suffering, letting grief be felt, and then softening into a wider, more relieving heart.

chapters.title: Two sides of love
chapters.description: Compassion and equanimity are presented as mutually supporting, with each correcting the distortions of the other.

chapters.title: Suffering wisely
chapters.description: He speaks about global dukkha, the danger of absorbing it in ways that corrode the heart, and the need to rest.

chapters.title: Local and practical care
chapters.description: A story about a politically engaged teacher leads into a suggestion to ask where love is most effective and local.

chapters.title: Closing practice instructions
chapters.description: He returns to the metta sequence, pairing taking on suffering with the 'as if' boundary and then releasing into equanimity.

title: Equanimity 3
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What happens when you stop trying to force experience into the right shape and instead learn to stay with pleasant, unpleasant, and the urge to react? The talk moves from definitions of equanimity into a practical map: balanced perception, less identification, and a widening of perspective that makes reactive stories less necessary. It then turns to how that balance ripens over time, how it can feel painful at first, and why even shame and jealousy may need to be met carefully rather than pushed away. The closing sections become more intimate and specific, with questions about difficult feelings, small acts of practice, mortality, and the support of another person’s calm gaze.
short_summary: A grounded exploration of equanimity as balanced, intimate non-reactivity, moving from theory and cited teachers into questions about shame, aversion, and how difficult feelings can be met without being overwhelmed.

chapters.title: Beginning with affect
chapters.description: Matthew starts with the raw fact that much of our suffering and social narration grows out of how we meet pleasant and unpleasant feeling.

chapters.title: What equanimity means
chapters.description: He lays out several definitions of equanimity, distinguishing it from numbness and showing how it widens perspective.

chapters.title: Not self-absorption
chapters.description: The talk turns to how suffering narrows us and how equanimity can soften the compulsion to fix, tweak, or brace against experience.

chapters.title: A workable sequence
chapters.description: Using Alyssa Mrazek’s four aspects of acceptance, he breaks the practice into acknowledging, allowing, non-judgment, and non-attachment.

chapters.title: What it is not
chapters.description: He warns against over-reading moments of experience, and clarifies that equanimity is not apathy, passivity, or tolerance of injustice.

chapters.title: Compassion with balance
chapters.description: Matthew shows how equanimity supports more effective compassion by reducing compulsivity, desperation, and the urge to force change.

chapters.title: Love and gratitude
chapters.description: In dialogue with the room, he describes radical permission as a form of love and cites Stephen Levine on acceptance as gratitude.

chapters.title: How it ripens
chapters.description: He answers a question about conditions for equanimity by pointing to causes and effects in the awakening factors, repeated practice, and the mind being gently kneaded by practice.

chapters.title: Shame, mortality, and support
chapters.description: The closing questions move into shame, jealousy, and other hard zones, ending with the need for patience, small openings, and sometimes the steady gaze of a spiritual friend.

title: Equanimity 2
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: When pleasant or unpleasant feelings start to feel self-relevant, the talk traces how quickly the mind turns them into a project: something to secure, fix, or defend. It then shifts toward a different response to dukkha and pain—not more control, but relaxing into sensory experience and seeing the push and pull as a flow of arising and passing. The emphasis stays practical and close to experience: notice the alarm, the urgency, the bracing, and see what loosens when those reactions are allowed to soften.
short_summary: A talk on equanimity that moves from self-relevance and alarm to relaxing control and letting experience flow.

chapters.title: Self-Relevance
chapters.description: Brensilver introduces how phenomena are evaluated through their significance to the project of the self.

chapters.title: Pleasant and Unpleasant
chapters.description: He describes how experiences feel pleasant when they support the project of life and unpleasant when they threaten it.

chapters.title: Alarm and Strategy
chapters.description: The talk turns to how alarm makes the present into a problem, pushing the mind toward control and disengagement from direct feeling.

chapters.title: What Equanimity Means
chapters.description: He reframes equanimity as dissolving the need to solve everything, allowing experience to be seen as arising and passing.

chapters.title: Desperation Around Pain
chapters.description: Brensilver names the panic that often arises with unpleasantness and distinguishes bodily pain from the larger prediction of ruin.

chapters.title: Relaxing the Bracing
chapters.description: He invites a relaxation of judgment, contraction, and the urgency to govern life moment by moment.

chapters.title: Letting Go Practice
chapters.description: The talk closes with a sustained repetition of letting go, emphasizing the felt release rather than a conceptual conclusion.

title: Equanimity 1
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Equanimity here is not presented as calm distance, but as a way of meeting the pressure of experience without trying to control it. The talk moves from a practical distinction between lecture and lived practice, into stories from retreat participants, and then into a sustained inquiry into how suffering, preference, helplessness, and love shape the heart. Near the end, the speaker turns to the Satipatthana formula and to feeling tone itself, arguing that equanimity is less about blankness than about softening our relationship to what arises.
short_summary: A wide-ranging talk on equanimity as the ongoing practice of meeting feeling, helplessness, and imperfection without grasping or pushing away.

chapters.title: What retreat teaches
chapters.description: Matthew opens by contrasting lecture with the experiential work of retreat and frames the day as practice with the problem of suffering.

chapters.title: First meanings of equanimity
chapters.description: He introduces equanimity through Michelle McDonald’s phrase and begins distinguishing it from preference, grit, and emotional flattening.

chapters.title: Softening instead of freezing
chapters.description: The talk develops equanimity as a heart that softens in the face of imperfection rather than hardening into grim endurance.

chapters.title: Helplessness and wise action
chapters.description: Using questions from participants, including prison work, politics, and family life, he explores equanimity as love in the face of helplessness and as a basis for response rather than reaction.

chapters.title: Sitting with experience
chapters.description: Matthew leads a long period of guided silence, inviting attention to the body, the room, and the models we build of self, world, and future.

chapters.title: Friction falls away
chapters.description: He returns to the theme of equanimity as a reduction in compulsive friction, especially when the mind no longer waits for pleasure to rescue it.

chapters.title: Love it, not escape it
chapters.description: A playful dialogue with the Buddha and a quotation from the Satipatthana Sutta sharpen the claim that mindfulness begins by setting down covetousness and displeasure.

chapters.title: Feeling at the center
chapters.description: Matthew closes by linking equanimity to de-reification, the move from story to contour, and the pervasive role of feeling tone in every moment of mind.

title: Dharmette: The mirage of self evaporates as the fires cool
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What happens when the urge to get free is still tangled with the very self that wants freedom? The talk follows a quote from Ajahn Sucitto into a meditation on cooling rather than struggle: nibbana as space, dispassion, and the easing of compulsive heat. Along the way it takes up Mara, evolutionary conditioning, and the way practice can shift from self-improvement to a quieter, less territorial care for life. It closes by asking how the self may reappear more innocently once it is no longer being endlessly curated.
short_summary: A reflection on nibbana as cooling and spaciousness, where the mirage of self fades not through force but through a selfless path of relinquishment and clearer seeing.

chapters.title: Ajahn Sucitto's frame
chapters.description: Matthew introduces Ajahn Sucitto's teaching on dispassion and cooling as the lens for the whole talk.

chapters.title: Practice turns inward
chapters.description: He reflects on how radical the path feels, especially for lay practitioners, and questions the wish to keep some of both samsara and nibbana.

chapters.title: Mara and conditioning
chapters.description: Using a brief Metzinger reference, he describes evolutionary conditioning, the reward system, and the self-model as forces that feed compulsivity.

chapters.title: Openness instead of denial
chapters.description: Matthew argues for respectful, radical openness to suffering rather than fighting or denying Mara, and names suffering as a kind of forgetting.

chapters.title: Space and cooling
chapters.description: He returns to nibbana as space in the mind and contrasts suffering's claustrophobia and heat with dispassion and tranquility that remain fully alive.

chapters.title: The self cannot own freedom
chapters.description: The talk turns to ego's tendency to colonize even spiritual practice, and then to the idea that freedom is subtraction, not an attainment.

chapters.title: What remains
chapters.description: With the image of the self as a hologram, he suggests that genuine dissolution of self leads to more care and a more open relation to life.

title: Guided Meditation: Freeing Experience
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What if freedom is not something the self gets, but something experience is freed from? The talk begins with that idea and then turns into a guided meditation on widening awareness, settling the body, and letting images, sounds, feelings, pain, and thoughts take their place without clinging. Again and again, it returns to the question of how much can be released in the breath, so that experience becomes less owned, less tense, and more impersonal.
short_summary: A guided meditation that uses the theme of “freeing everything else” to open awareness, soften ownership, and let experience arise without clinging.

chapters.title: Freedom Reframed
chapters.description: The talk opens with Gil Fronsdal’s line and a Majjhima Nikaya reference to frame liberation as freeing experience rather than securing a self.

chapters.title: Settling the Body
chapters.description: He guides posture, lengthening the spine, grounding into the earth, and arriving with the breath.

chapters.title: Including What Appears
chapters.description: The practice expands into letting experience take its place in awareness without being pushed away or caught.

chapters.title: Freeing Experience
chapters.description: He names images, sounds, pain, tranquility, feelings, and thoughts as things to be freed rather than possessed.

chapters.title: Less Possession
chapters.description: The meditation turns toward the strain of possessiveness and the release of the sense that experience must be held as mine.

chapters.title: Impersonal Arising
chapters.description: Arising and passing come to feel less personal, with awareness taking the foreground and continuity of self loosening.

chapters.title: Equanimity and Control
chapters.description: He reflects on control, delusion, and how equanimity lets the self thin out.

chapters.title: Resting in Awareness
chapters.description: The final minutes are mostly silence and bell chimes, allowing the meditation to settle without further instruction.

title: Dharmette: Remembering Ann Buck
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: A remembrance of a friend becomes a meditation on how practice actually shows itself in ordinary life: at a stoplight, in a hospital room, on retreat, and in the long arc of aging. The talk moves from Dylan’s “knocking on heaven’s door,” to a brief reading from Timothy Snyder, to stories of Ann Buck’s fierce warmth, her blunt encouragement to love yourself, and the way her Dharma confidence stayed intact through loss, illness, and time. It ends by widening grief into a question about what the dead would want from the living, with a poem by James Fenton about memory, generosity, and continuing well.
short_summary: A quiet remembrance of Ann Buck that turns personal grief into reflections on practice, self-love, aging, and how to honor the dead.

chapters.title: Morning, Dylan, mortality
chapters.description: Matthew begins with a morning stoplight, a Dylan lyric, and the mood of mortality that frames the rest of the talk.

chapters.title: Ann’s passing and example
chapters.description: He introduces Ann Buck’s death, her long practice, and the strange coexistence of tragedy, humor, and perspective.

chapters.title: Life in the ordinary
chapters.description: A sequence of small, vivid moments shows how practice meets daily life, from coffee to a bright pink bike.

chapters.title: Cling, then let go
chapters.description: Matthew recalls retreat memories with Ann and a shared recognition of attachment and release.

chapters.title: Her direct kindness
chapters.description: He describes Ann’s fierce clarity, including her strong encouragement to love oneself, and the trust that came with her words.

chapters.title: Dharma through conversation
chapters.description: Their phone calls and shared conversations are presented as naturally infused with Dharma and grounded support.

chapters.title: Mind, aging, and refuge
chapters.description: Matthew reflects on Ann’s practice lineage and the steadiness of a mind described as empty, radiant, and responsive.

chapters.title: What the dead might want
chapters.description: He closes with a James Fenton poem that turns grief toward memory, generosity, and continued life.

title: Guided Meditation: Resting in Goodness
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The talk starts from a simple instruction: let goodness itself soothe the heart. From there, it moves through posture, attention, and the habit of making meditation into a performance, then loosens that pressure by inviting forgiveness toward the mind and trust in awareness and love. The emphasis keeps returning to resting rather than striving, and to noticing that what is already good and enjoyable in experience can be felt more directly.
short_summary: A guided meditation on relaxing the pressure to perform, trusting awareness, and resting in the goodness already present in experience.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: He welcomes the group and invites a simple settling into posture and presence.

chapters.title: Goodness as Soothe
chapters.description: The meditation begins by letting ordinary goodness calm and soften the heart.

chapters.title: Attention and Agitation
chapters.description: He contrasts the usual focus on breath and body with allowing goodness to ease worry and reveal what remains.

chapters.title: Pressure on Self
chapters.description: The talk turns toward how meditation can become a self-improvement project and how the self can be relaxed.

chapters.title: Meditation by Itself
chapters.description: He encourages surrendering the sense of self to awareness, then deep forgiveness of the mind and trust in awareness and love.

chapters.title: Clinging and Aliveness
chapters.description: He points to the goodness of mind as independent of effort, and explores how clinging narrows experience while non-clinging feels more alive.

chapters.title: Enjoy What Can Be
chapters.description: Near the end, he invites listeners to enjoy whatever is available in experience and let the practice rest in simple appreciation.

title: Dharmette: To Care and Not to Care Simultaneously
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: How can one care deeply without getting trapped in strain, grief, or attachment? The talk begins with T.S. Eliot’s line “teach us to care and not to care,” then works through the difference between caring sequentially and the more difficult question of caring and not caring at once. From there it moves into emptiness as a thinning of perception, using a sutta image of village, wilderness, earth, and then even subtler absence, before closing with the picture of consciousness as light that no longer lands anywhere and so finds no sorrow.
short_summary: A meditation on the tension between compassion and equanimity, using Eliot and sutta imagery to explore what it might mean to care and not care simultaneously.

chapters.title: Eliot and the question
chapters.description: Matthew opens with T.S. Eliot’s Ash Wednesday and uses its line about caring and not caring to frame the talk’s central tension.

chapters.title: Love and rest
chapters.description: He describes care as costly and argues for a rhythm between compassion and rest, rather than a heart that is either closed or overextended.

chapters.title: Beyond sequence
chapters.description: The talk shifts from alternating states to the possibility of caring and not caring simultaneously, which he presents as one way emptiness can be understood.

chapters.title: Refining perception
chapters.description: Using the Lesser Discourse on Emptiness, he traces perception becoming less agitated, from village to wilderness to earth, with fewer coarse forms appearing.

chapters.title: Less and less
chapters.description: He extends the emptiness teaching into subtler states of perception, including the sense of infinite space and a mind less dependent on ordinary sensory life.

chapters.title: Where mind does not land
chapters.description: Matthew turns to the sunlight simile from another sutta to describe consciousness that does not land anywhere, connecting that freedom with the end of sorrow.

chapters.title: Quiet closing
chapters.description: He offers the teaching for reflection, then closes with a brief farewell and well-wishing.

title: Guided Meditation: Grounding and Opening
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The talk begins with simple instructions to settle in, then keeps returning to the body as the place where attention can land: posture, belly, bones, gravity, and the feeling of “taking your seat.” A repeated image of the Buddha touching the earth becomes the central grounding move, shifting practice away from the head and heart and back into embodied presence. From there, the guidance widens to include emotional activation, letting it be bathed in awareness and patience rather than measured against a plan for how life or meditation should unfold. Near the end, the emphasis softens into a release of performance, preference, and the need to get it right.
short_summary: A guided meditation that uses the image of touching the earth to help attention settle into the belly, bones, and grounded awareness of the body.

chapters.title: Beginning Together
chapters.description: He invites the group to practice and settle into the meditation.

chapters.title: Posture and Belly
chapters.description: The practice begins with finding posture and bringing attention down into the belly, gravity, and the bones.

chapters.title: Touching the Earth
chapters.description: He uses the Buddha’s earth-touching gesture as a repeated image for grounding, asking listeners to know themselves as earth and breathe down into the ground.

chapters.title: Settling and Listening
chapters.description: After the initial grounding, the practice continues in a quieter register of resting, listening, and staying with bodily presence.

chapters.title: Affect in the Body
chapters.description: He turns toward emotional activation as bodily circuits that can be met with awareness and patience.

chapters.title: Dropping Expectations
chapters.description: The teaching shifts toward releasing hidden models of how life or meditation should unfold and using everything to become more alive.

chapters.title: Open Practice
chapters.description: The meditation continues in a broad, steady way, supporting simple presence without needing to control the experience.

chapters.title: No Performance
chapters.description: He closes by setting aside pleasure, preferences, and the pressure to perform.

title: Dharmette: Unsustainable Trajectories & Love
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The talk begins with a hard question: what do we do when our lives, and our society, seem to be on unsustainable trajectories? From there, the speaker moves through anxiety, anger, and a wary brush with nihilism, then turns back toward a practice response that is less about fixing everything than about not adding more harm. A passage from the Dhammapada and a brief story of rangers rescuing a goose on Mount Shasta bring the talk into a quieter register, where care, stewardship, and love are treated as the only durable way forward.
short_summary: Starting from the sense that life and society may be on an unsustainable path, the talk moves from anxiety and anger toward a steadier practice of non-harming, wisdom, compassion, and care.

chapters.title: Unsustainable beginnings
chapters.description: Matthew starts with the Buddha’s realization that his own way of life could not last and draws a parallel to the instability people feel now.

chapters.title: A larger trajectory
chapters.description: He expands the idea of unsustainability from one life to society, technology, inequality, and climate.

chapters.title: Anger and confusion
chapters.description: The talk turns toward evidence, overconfidence, and the way anger can simplify the world into agents and causes.

chapters.title: Powerlessness and nihilism
chapters.description: Matthew names the emotional strain of the moment, including the temptation to collapse into futility or escape.

chapters.title: Freedom and hope
chapters.description: He contrasts freedom independent of conditions with the need, at times, to keep hope alive without clinging to the wrong things.

chapters.title: Planting long seeds
chapters.description: Hope is widened beyond a single lifetime, and love is framed as patient seed-planting rather than immediate rescue.

chapters.title: Back to fundamentals
chapters.description: He returns to the Dhammapada’s simple instruction to do no evil, do what is skillful, and purify the mind.

chapters.title: The goose on Shasta
chapters.description: A rescued goose becomes a gentle story of effort, care, tenderness, and the necessity of stewardship.

title: Guided Meditation: Putting Down the World
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What happens if practice asks us to stop trading this moment for a better one? The talk centers on a radical gesture of "putting down the world"—setting down greed, distress, autobiography, and the compulsion to manage time or outcome—while noticing the resistance that can feel like abandonment. From that stillness, the emphasis shifts to a fuller heart, clearer action, and a more intimate love for the world as it is.
short_summary: A guided meditation on resting without transaction, putting down greed and distress, and finding that this surrender can deepen love and clarity.

chapters.title: Opening reflection
chapters.description: Brensilver opens with a brief reflection on practice as a radical surrender, putting the world down without abandoning care.

chapters.title: Settling the posture
chapters.description: The instruction turns to posture, alertness, brightness, and a non-tensed way of arriving.

chapters.title: Putting the world down
chapters.description: He names greed and distress with respect to the world, and explores the fear that letting go means neglecting what matters.

chapters.title: Autobiography and care
chapters.description: The talk deepens into the density of self-story, time, and compulsive care, pointing toward a deeper love beneath them.

chapters.title: Simple immediacy
chapters.description: Attention is guided toward the present-moment texture of sound, sensation, and breath, with less effort and less making.

chapters.title: No bartering
chapters.description: He cautions against using practice as a means to get somewhere, or as a buffer from intimacy with life.

chapters.title: Falling into the present
chapters.description: The meditation widens into resting without trading the moment away, and repeatedly returns to presence as something that can be entered rather than achieved.

chapters.title: Love after letting go
chapters.description: In the closing teaching, he returns to the idea that laying down the world can deepen love, reduce hatred, and make ordinary helpfulness more natural.

title: Dharmette: Truth & Lies in the US in 2025
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What happens when the impulse to correct lies turns into rage, and that rage starts to feel like its own trap? The talk begins with childhood anger at hypocrisy, then moves through suffering, honesty, and the damage deception does to shared reality, drawing on a Theravada commentary, Harry Frankfurt’s distinction between lying and bullshit, and a remark from Thomas Metzinger on truthfulness. From there it turns inward: the struggle to stay sane in a deceptive political moment, the pull toward outrage, and a quieter practice of treasuring truth without making one’s peace depend on the world honoring it.
short_summary: A meditation on truthfulness, self-deception, and political outrage that asks how to stay rooted in reality without getting swallowed by anger.

chapters.title: Hypocrisy and early anger
chapters.description: He begins with a childhood sensitivity to hypocrisy and the feeling of being mobilized by lies and denial.

chapters.title: Honesty as moral care
chapters.description: He links truth-telling to empathy, trauma recovery, and the moral responsibility to help others know what is real.

chapters.title: Lies and bullshit
chapters.description: He turns to Buddhist ethics and Harry Frankfurt to distinguish deliberate lying from indifference to truth.

chapters.title: Truth in practice
chapters.description: He uses Thomas Metzinger to connect scientific rigor and spiritual practice through a shared commitment to veracity.

chapters.title: Political outrage cycle
chapters.description: He names the current U.S. political moment and describes how anger, evidence-gathering, and self-righteousness can become a closed loop.

chapters.title: Treasure the truth
chapters.description: He offers a practical shift: speak truth as best one can, stop depending on the world to reward truthfulness, and give up the debate.

chapters.title: Seeing self-deception
chapters.description: He reflects on motivated reasoning and Nietzsche's line about truth and strength, turning it into a wish to become more able to bear truth.

chapters.title: Sangha and letting go
chapters.description: He ends by naming sangha as a place where reality can be faced together and by reminding listeners to let go before clinging is broken.

title: Guided Meditation: Breathing into Compassion
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Starting from James Baldwin’s idea that pain matters only insofar as it can connect us with everyone else alive, the talk turns personal suffering into the ground of compassion. From there, the practice moves into a guided settling with breath and body: sensing the body as a field, easing attention, and letting breathing unify what feels divided or excluded. The emphasis stays simple and steady—rest with sensation, let pain become less private, and allow compassion to widen without forcing the details.
short_summary: A guided meditation that uses breath, body sensation, and James Baldwin’s insight on pain to open compassion and connection.

chapters.title: Baldwin and pain
chapters.description: Matthew introduces James Baldwin’s reflection on suffering and frames personal pain as a way to connect with others.

chapters.title: Sense the body
chapters.description: The meditation shifts into directly knowing experience as sensation and settling into the body as the main field of practice.

chapters.title: Breath across the body
chapters.description: Attention follows the breath to see where it reaches, then opens to the whole body without strain.

chapters.title: Simple resting
chapters.description: He invites a relaxed, receptive posture of attention, with imagery and the inner gaze resting quietly as sensation unifies.

chapters.title: Pain as connection
chapters.description: The practice returns to suffering and reframes it as something that can connect us to others and depersonalize samsara.

chapters.title: Compassion opens wider
chapters.description: Compassion is encouraged to become broad and practical, without getting stuck in details or the sense that suffering is too vast.

chapters.title: Breath as love
chapters.description: The meditation deepens into a sense that breathing can convey love through the body and honor the shared condition of sentience.

chapters.title: Shared life and closing
chapters.description: He ends by pointing to how intertwined lives are and closes with a brief recitation and the sound of the bell.

title: Dharmette: more of our personality in freedom than in our suffering
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What if suffering is the thing that makes us feel most like a fixed character, while freedom is what reveals our actual particularity? The talk begins with Tolstoy’s famous line about unhappy families, then turns it inside out: suffering may come in many forms, but it is also strangely repetitive, while liberated life shows up as spontaneity, quirkiness, and distinctiveness. From there, the speaker moves through empathy, the cessation of suffering, Winnicott’s spontaneous gesture, and a closing reflection on how to meet others as already free, or at least not far from it.
short_summary: A reflection on how suffering feels uniquely personal, while freedom is where individuality, spontaneity, and true selfhood become visible.

chapters.title: Tolstoy Reversed
chapters.description: He opens with Tolstoy and turns the famous line around to show that suffering may be more alike across people than it first appears.

chapters.title: Pain Feels Unique
chapters.description: He lingers on how suffering can feel like a private brand, while also pointing to its shared, disconnecting character.

chapters.title: Meeting Others Carefully
chapters.description: Using Carl Rogers, he describes empathic attunement as entering another’s world without losing perspective, and notes that another person’s freedom can feel surprising and instructive.

chapters.title: What Freedom Looks Like
chapters.description: He turns to the Buddha’s emphasis on cessation and asks what a life freed from greed, hate, and delusion might actually look like in ordinary personality and ethics.

chapters.title: True Self and Gesture
chapters.description: With Winnicott, he contrasts role-playing and melodrama with spontaneous gesture, suggesting that freedom lets deeper particularity come forward.

chapters.title: Already Enlightened
chapters.description: He quotes Bruce Tift on treating people as already enlightened and uses that line to reframe how we see ourselves and others.

chapters.title: Closing Questions
chapters.description: He ends by inviting reflection on how practice reveals both our own freedom and the uniqueness of others.

title: Attention and Anicca, Awareness and Anatta
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What happens when attention is treated as love, and then that same attention is turned back to expose impermanence and not-self? The talk moves from a humorous story about trying to “fix” a partner, through Simone Weil, William James, and retreat practice, into a careful distinction between attention and awareness. From there it asks whether practice is really about control at all, or about learning to rest in what cannot be governed, until the sense of a centered self begins to loosen.
short_summary: A quiet, searching talk on attention, awareness, and the way practice gradually softens the grip of selfhood.

chapters.title: From Fixing to Listening
chapters.description: Matthew opens with a comic story about trying to solve a partner’s problems before turning to the simpler discipline of truly listening.

chapters.title: Attention as Love
chapters.description: He links listening, meditation, and love, then introduces Simone Weil’s idea that attention can function like prayer.

chapters.title: Unmixed Attention
chapters.description: Using Weil more fully, he describes attention purified of craving and selfing as something that sanctifies ordinary experience.

chapters.title: Right Now, Like This
chapters.description: Ajahn Sumedho’s phrase and reflections on conscious experience lead into a contrast between what we feel and the deeper currents beneath awareness.

chapters.title: What Awareness Means
chapters.description: A story about asking Shinzen Young what awareness is opens a discussion of attention, awareness, and the different emphases of Buddhist lineages.

chapters.title: How Attention Shapes Experience
chapters.description: He draws on the Buddha, William James, and attention science to describe attention as the condition through which phenomena, concentration, and training arise.

chapters.title: Attention and Impermanence
chapters.description: The talk turns to anicca, the costs of effortful practice, and the way attention can strengthen the sense of an agent at the center of experience.

chapters.title: Awareness Without Control
chapters.description: A child’s cry in the night leads into a gentler account of awareness as dependent on conditions rather than personal will, and of love as seeing the three characteristics clearly.

chapters.title: Surrendering the Center
chapters.description: The close shifts into a deeper relinquishment of the narrative self, with a philosophical note on silent, non-conceptual awareness and the innocence of self-reappearance.

title: Guided meditation
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The talk begins with settling the body and finding whatever in the moment feels even slightly good or soothing—especially the breath, the pause, and the relief of simply stopping. From there, it explores how equanimity lets attention rest without immediately turning toward worry, planning, or trying to rearrange the world, and how that makes room for a more open, less snagged way of being with experience. The practice keeps widening: the heart is offered to the moment, the aperture of attention opens, and what felt heavy can start to feel lighter and more spacious.
short_summary: A guided meditation on settling into stillness, using the breath and equanimity to let attention open and soften around experience.

chapters.title: Settling the body
chapters.description: He invites listeners to find a posture and arrive in stillness in whatever position is available.

chapters.title: Finding what feels good
chapters.description: The guidance turns toward noticing small pleasantness in stopping and using that as a light anchor.

chapters.title: Less urgency, more equanimity
chapters.description: He reflects on worry, planning, and the ordinary pull of concerns, framing equanimity as staying with what is less urgent.

chapters.title: Breath as refuge
chapters.description: Attention narrows to the breath as something soothing and naturally regulating.

chapters.title: Mindfulness and letting go
chapters.description: He links mindfulness to setting aside greed and distress toward the world, and to turning inward without trying to fix the outside.

chapters.title: Offering the heart up
chapters.description: The meditation deepens into repeated reminders to meet the moment with trust and openness, without needing a particular outcome.

chapters.title: Widening attention
chapters.description: He invites a broader aperture of attention and explores how awareness can soften the heaviness objects and thoughts take on.

chapters.title: Lightness and space
chapters.description: The teaching returns to spaciousness, including a lighter relationship to autobiography and to attention moving without snagging.

chapters.title: Keep opening
chapters.description: The close offers a simple, repeated instruction to keep winding or opening, ending the practice gently.

title: Dharmette: It's Complicated and It's Simple
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The talk begins by taking stock of how complicated life really is: the brain, the world, and even our own motives resist tidy explanation. From that wide view, it turns toward a much simpler frame—love and heartbreak—and asks what changes when we stop getting lost in the wrong categories and look instead at suffering, peace, and the limits of control. Along the way, the speaker uses a retreat student’s insight, a Buddha teaching, and a line from Leonard Cohen to show how practice can meet life’s unavoidable tenderness with more honesty and grace.
short_summary: A wide-ranging reflection on complexity that settles into the simple, recurring realities of love, heartbreak, and the practice of meeting them clearly.

chapters.title: Life Is Complicated
chapters.description: He begins with the complexity of the world, our lives, and the systems we inhabit, setting up the talk’s opening tension.

chapters.title: Brains and Networks
chapters.description: Matthew uses Wolf Singer’s remarks to show how brain science keeps becoming more abstract and relational rather than simpler.

chapters.title: Stories We Tell
chapters.description: He turns to how we explain ourselves, noting that our stories about motives and identity may not match the actual causes of our actions.

chapters.title: The Reach of the Cosmos
chapters.description: A brief cosmology passage widens the frame further, ending with the sense that there is much we will never know.

chapters.title: Love and Heartbreak
chapters.description: A retreat student’s simple insight becomes the bridge from complexity to a more direct understanding of experience.

chapters.title: Right Distinctions
chapters.description: Matthew invokes the Buddha’s teaching that confusion comes from the wrong categories, while practice points toward suffering and peace.

chapters.title: No Cure for Love
chapters.description: Using Leonard Cohen, he reflects on love’s inevitability and heartbreak’s inseparability, especially in close relationships.

chapters.title: Yielding to Time
chapters.description: He closes by naming the need to let life be offered back to time, holding both aspiration and tenderness without losing sight of either.

title: Guided Meditation: Mostly Quiet Sit
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided sit moves from settling the body into a quiet, spacious attention that does not force experience to become anything else. The talk keeps returning to simple instructions: attend where it feels appropriate, let go, and not be afraid of silence. Along the way, the speaker softens the practice with the image of the sangha as a field of goodwill, then closes by framing the whole sit as a kind of radical permission for conditions to be as they are, without turning that into passivity in every moment.
short_summary: A mostly quiet guided meditation on letting go, resting in silence, and offering present conditions radical permission.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: After the welcome, the practice turns toward sitting and sensing where attention feels appropriate.

chapters.title: Personalizing Practice
chapters.description: Matthew frames the Dharma as something we learn from the Buddha and lineage, but also have to discover in our own life.

chapters.title: Letting Go
chapters.description: The guidance softens into a quiet release, with an image of the Buddha whispering to let go and not fear silence.

chapters.title: Sangha Support
chapters.description: The silent presence of the sangha is invited as a field of goodwill that can soothe the heart and make letting go feel safer.

chapters.title: Radical Permission
chapters.description: The meditation opens into a deeper acceptance of present conditions, alongside a reminder that wisdom later determines the balance between receptivity and action.

chapters.title: Closing Bells
chapters.description: The sit ends with a short sequence of bells and quiet closing space.

title: Dharmette: The Divine Abodes (5 of 5) Equanimity
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: How do we keep caring without being worn down by the very fact that suffering is so large and so hard to govern? The talk develops equanimity as a kind of deep rest for the heart: still but not rigid, able to hold compassion without collapsing into compulsion, exhaustion, or helplessness. Along the way, it moves through the cost of caring, the danger of nihilism, and the middle path between thinking nothing matters and thinking everything matters. The result is a quiet case for refining love so it becomes more sustainable and more potent.
short_summary: A reflective talk on equanimity as deep rest for the heart, balancing compassion, helplessness, and the risk of nihilism.

chapters.title: Opening gratitude
chapters.description: Matthew begins with a warm greeting and thanks the many people whose support makes the gathering possible.

chapters.title: Ease and release
chapters.description: He introduces equanimity through Ajahn Buddhadasa and reflects on the wish for beings to find ease as suffering keeps displacing suffering.

chapters.title: Still, not rigid
chapters.description: Using Ajahn Sucitto's phrasing, he describes equanimity as quiet, loving, and respectful, then sets it alongside the costly reality of compassion.

chapters.title: The cost of caring
chapters.description: A research study on compassion is used to explore why caring can feel taxing and why equanimity matters for making love sustainable.

chapters.title: Heart at rest
chapters.description: Matthew compares equanimity to sleep for the heart, warning against chasing stimulation and suggesting that rest does not require collapse.

chapters.title: Caring and not caring
chapters.description: With T. S. Eliot and a discussion of the Buddha's concern about nihilism, he frames the practice as a middle way between emptiness and over-significance.

chapters.title: Purifying compassion
chapters.description: He describes equanimity as a cross-check that refines compassion, reducing compulsive or self-righteous forms of care.

chapters.title: Helplessness and goodwill
chapters.description: The talk closes by naming helplessness directly and returning to a practical posture of resting without apathy, then ending with well-wishes and announcements.

title: Guided Meditation: Self-acceptance
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: How do you stop sorting yourself into acceptable and unacceptable parts long enough to meet your life with kindness? The talk moves from a short settling into a clear invitation to rest in the body, then widen into the sense that a person is shaped by countless forces—history, conditioning, joy, and sorrow—that cannot be fully controlled. From there it turns toward a radical yes to experience: not to excuse harm or avoid change, but to let acceptance end shame and make room for compassion, equanimity, and a simple blessing practice of “may I be happy, may I be safe, may I be at peace.”
short_summary: A guided meditation on self-acceptance that moves from body awareness into forgiving conditioning, ending shame, and resting in a simple metta phrase.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: The meditation begins by inviting stillness and an attentive arrival into practice.

chapters.title: Body and Awareness
chapters.description: Attention is poured into the body and the body is encouraged to feel bright and vivid with awareness.

chapters.title: Kindness for Conditioning
chapters.description: The teaching widens into compassion for the forces, seen and unseen, that have shaped this moment and this life.

chapters.title: Accepting My Life
chapters.description: He frames acceptance as a way of being with the whole of one's life, including suffering, good fortune, and helplessness.

chapters.title: A Radical Yes
chapters.description: The talk turns to the habit of sorting ourselves into acceptable and unacceptable parts and asks for a complete stop to that pattern.

chapters.title: End the Inner Game
chapters.description: Brensilver encourages dropping self-criticism, shame, and the constant behavioral correction project, and simply opening to the totality of what one is.

chapters.title: Loving-Kindness Closing
chapters.description: The practice closes with repeated phrases of goodwill: may I be happy, safe, protected, and at peace.

title: Dharmette: The Divine Abodes (4 of 5) Sympathetic Joy
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Mudita, or sympathetic joy, is approached here as something you can’t force and don’t need to fake. The talk moves from the difficulty of delighting in another’s good fortune to a practical account of what gets in the way: ownership, comparison, envy, and the ego’s habit of taking in every bit of goodness as its own. Along the way, the speaker uses a sutta from the Anguttara Nikaya and brief references to Joseph Epstein and Matthieu Ricard to sharpen the point that other people’s happiness does not deprive us of anything; it can actually free the heart.
short_summary: A quiet, practical talk on sympathetic joy, exploring how envy loosens when the heart stops treating others’ happiness as a threat.

chapters.title: Arriving at Mudita
chapters.description: Brensilver introduces sympathetic joy and notes that the morning practice had already moved through other territory before settling here.

chapters.title: Joy Cannot Be Forced
chapters.description: He defines mudita, distinguishes it from forced smiling, and emphasizes taking time with the physiology rather than pretending.

chapters.title: Resentment and the Sutta
chapters.description: He turns to the Anguttara Nikaya and a personal example to show mudita as an antidote to resentment.

chapters.title: Joy Beyond Scarcity
chapters.description: Brensilver contrasts worldly happiness with the deeper availability of joy through others' happiness and shared laughter.

chapters.title: Letting Down the Burden
chapters.description: He explores how joy can feel irresponsible or disloyal to grief, and argues for an emotional life that can hold several states without strain.

chapters.title: What Blocks Joy
chapters.description: The talk shifts to the obstacles to mudita—ownership, possessiveness, comparison, and envy—and how ego tries to absorb all goodness.

chapters.title: Jealousy and Envy
chapters.description: He distinguishes jealousy from envy, briefly cites evolutionary psychology, and then uses Matthieu Ricard to frame envy as wounded self-importance.

chapters.title: Sufficient and Unthreatened
chapters.description: He closes by describing the freedom that comes with sufficiency: other people's success no longer threatens the self and becomes a source of delight.

title: Guided meditation: Affect and the Lure of Thought
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: How do you stop giving thought the last word when it feels like it is holding loose ends, emotions, and unfinished business together? This guided meditation moves between a gentle settling and a sharper "start on a dime" stance, inviting a quick renunciation of distraction and a willingness to feel the vulnerability that can show up when thinking is no longer used as a pacifier. The practice keeps returning to the body, emotional activation, and equanimity, showing how urgency softens when attention stays close to direct experience.
short_summary: A guided meditation on dropping into the body, meeting emotional activation, and letting go of the lure of thought with decisiveness and equanimity.

chapters.title: Settling into practice
chapters.description: He begins by describing a gentle way of entering meditation and how the act of getting in can matter as much as where one arrives.

chapters.title: Start on a dime
chapters.description: Using Shinzen Young’s advice, he contrasts soft settling with a more immediate, decisive turn away from distraction.

chapters.title: Putting thoughts down
chapters.description: He names the sense of incompletion that can make thoughts feel urgent, and invites the listener to set them aside anyway.

chapters.title: Renunciation as feeling
chapters.description: Renunciation is framed not as suppression but as the willingness to feel the egoic wound, uncertainty, and ambiguity without using thought as a pacifier.

chapters.title: Body and emotion
chapters.description: The guidance shifts toward breathing, bodily awareness, and tending carefully to emotional circuits that insist thinking is necessary.

chapters.title: Equanimity and urgency
chapters.description: He explains how meeting emotional activation with equanimity can make everything feel less urgent.

chapters.title: This is your life
chapters.description: The talk turns toward presence in ordinary life, emphasizing that practice is not postponed until later and that this moment is already the whole field.

chapters.title: Release of self
chapters.description: He closes by pointing to a deepening intimacy with experience that requires loosening self-clinging and resting in what he calls the wondrous dark.

title: Guided Meditation: Attention and Love
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation begins by settling again and again, then drops attention into the chest and belly to feel the body as grounded, rooted, and in contact with the earth. From that steadiness, the talk turns toward a quieter question: how attention can reveal what we love, what matters, and how to meet life with care rather than aversion. The practice ends by filling out the body with heart energy, softening into well-wishing, and a direct reminder to breathe and feel love instead of feeding hatred.
short_summary: A guided meditation on settling into the body, letting attention reveal love, and turning from hatred toward heartfulness.

chapters.title: Opening and Settling
chapters.description: After a brief welcome, the talk moves into silence and the first instructions for settling into practice.

chapters.title: Arriving in the Body
chapters.description: Attention is invited to settle as if for the first time, preparing for a more embodied practice.

chapters.title: Chest, Breath, Belly
chapters.description: The meditation moves through the heart center, the lungs, and then down into the belly as a place of groundedness.

chapters.title: Earth and Rootedness
chapters.description: Breath is used to connect more deeply with the earth element and the steadiness of being rooted.

chapters.title: Attention Reveals Love
chapters.description: The teaching shifts from body awareness to the idea that careful attention helps reveal what we love and care about.

chapters.title: Patience with Longing
chapters.description: Rather than forcing answers, the practice emphasizes patience and letting the poignancy of being born arise on its own.

chapters.title: Heart Energy in the Body
chapters.description: From groundedness, the heart energy is invited to fill the body, with emphasis on tenderness, flexibility, and wishing ourselves well.

chapters.title: Care and the Wider Field
chapters.description: The reflection broadens into ways attention can express care for one’s life, with the meditation holding space for what arises.

chapters.title: Love over Hatred
chapters.description: A poetic sequence reframes hatred as something brittle and unnecessary, ending with the invitation to breathe and feel love directly.

title: Dharmette; The Divine Abodes (3 of 5): Compassion
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What does it mean to love suffering without trying to control it? The talk begins with dukkha as something obvious and yet still hard to see, then turns to compassion as love in the face of pain, shaped by wisdom, grief, and the complexity of dependent arising. Along the way, it questions easy fixes, emotional enmeshment, and advice-giving, and settles into a quieter sense that compassion becomes steadier as we become more free.
short_summary: A talk on compassion that moves from the shock of dukkha to a wiser, steadier love that can meet suffering without clinging or bypassing.

chapters.title: Suffering Is Everywhere
chapters.description: He opens by naming dukkha as both obvious and strangely hard to fully see.

chapters.title: Deep and Hard to See
chapters.description: A Buddha quotation frames suffering as something that becomes clearer the closer we look.

chapters.title: The Question of Love
chapters.description: A grief poem and reflection on how to respond turn the talk toward love as an unresolved, living question.

chapters.title: What Compassion Is
chapters.description: Compassion is defined as love in the face of suffering, informed by wisdom and a more careful diagnosis of causes.

chapters.title: Loss Reveals Impermanence
chapters.description: Katherine Schultz’s reflections on her father’s death deepen the contemplation of anicca and the pervasive reach of loss.

chapters.title: Not Enmeshment
chapters.description: He distinguishes compassion from emotional contagion, insistence, and the wish to control another’s suffering.

chapters.title: Grief Held by Love
chapters.description: Compassion is presented as intimate, non-demanding, and willing to grieve without making grief the final word.

chapters.title: Wise and Steady Love
chapters.description: A Thich Nhat Hanh line serves as a checkpoint for the role of wisdom, equanimity, and inner freedom in making compassion sustainable.

title: Dharmette: Mindfulness: Acceptance and Change
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Is mindfulness about accepting what is here, or changing what is here?
