chapters.description: Matthew distinguishes harmful self-clinging from self-love and argues that kindness toward oneself supports insight.

chapters.title: Beyond Paradox
chapters.description: He explains why the subject feels paradoxical, then reframes self and not-self as experiences rather than metaphysical claims.

chapters.title: Buddha and Pragmatism
chapters.description: Using Vacchagotta’s question and Than Geoff’s comment, he sets aside speculative answers in favor of the end of suffering.

chapters.title: Ego and Development
chapters.description: A quote from Jack Engler helps separate psychological growth from spiritual language that turns ego into an enemy.

chapters.title: Mindfulness as Healing
chapters.description: He describes closing the eyes, meeting whatever arises, and letting practice digest memory, pain, and family history.

chapters.title: Suffering in the Self
chapters.description: Drawing on the Buddha and his own retreat stories, Matthew shows how self-clinging aims at safety, status, and feeling good.

chapters.title: Rumi and Sumedho
chapters.description: Rumi’s poem and Ajahn Sumedho’s line about personality and enlightenment shift the talk toward a freer stance.

chapters.title: Loving It to Death
chapters.description: He turns to subtle selfing, the sense of a home base of me, and closes with Norman Fisher on how practice quietly remakes a life.

title: Working with Anger: Part 1
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What if anger is both natural and deeply misleading: a force that can sometimes signal injustice, yet so easily hardens into a story that feels absolutely true? The talk moves from listeners’ questions about outbursts, suppression, self-anger, and being around other people’s anger into a careful distinction between what happens and how we respond, and into the question of how to meet anger without rehearsing it. Drawing on Thich Nhat Hanh, Ajahn Chah, the Dalai Lama, Shantideva, and a Buddha image of anger’s “honeyed tip and poisoned root,” the speaker keeps returning to one practical point: notice the tangle, see the seduction, and take good care of the heat before it takes over.
short_summary: A grounded exploration of anger as natural, seductive, and workable, with attention to how to notice it, not rehearse it, and avoid turning it into identity.

chapters.title: Opening questions
chapters.description: Listener questions set up the talk around acted-out anger, suppression, and the experience of being around other people's anger.

chapters.title: Not rehearsing anger
chapters.description: Matthew introduces Thich Nhat Hanh's warning against rehearsing anger while still avoiding suppression.

chapters.title: Impact and response
chapters.description: He explores the gap between what happens to us and our reaction, and the search for a middle path between bottling anger and acting it out.

chapters.title: Responsibility without self-blame
chapters.description: The talk turns toward self-anger, the pain of greater mindfulness, and the reminder that we are responsible for our problems without being identical with them.

chapters.title: Not an angry person
chapters.description: Using Ajahn Chah, Matthew questions the identity story of being an angry person and places anger within the wider context of suffering and not-self.

chapters.title: Natural, but costly
chapters.description: He acknowledges that anger can sometimes be useful, especially around injustice, while also describing its limits and its role in human and historical suffering.

chapters.title: Shantideva and patience
chapters.description: A Shantideva passage on patience deepens the case that anger erodes trust, safety, and virtue, and Pema Chodron's reading softens the tone into practical self-admonition.

chapters.title: Seeing anger directly
chapters.description: Matthew describes anger's 'honeyed tip and poisoned root,' then explains how to untangle thought, feeling, and self-image in practice.

chapters.title: Closing reflection
chapters.description: He ends by inviting direct mindfulness of anger, followed by a short period of quiet and a closing blessing toward non-harming and peace.
